text: Hello, I'm sharing a colon stricture. This is a elderly patient who is a very heavy smoker who has developed a symptomatic colon stricture and progressive constipation, had never needed to be admitted to the hospital, but not able to pass a colonoscope and presents for surgery.
The surgeon observes the dilated proximal bowel and prepares to decompress it using a two-oh silk suture to create a purse string in the area planned for resection.

I was a little concerned in this case, given the smoking history, um, and the size discrepancy between the proximal and distal bowel about making an anastomosis. Um, But what I realized here is that the patient essentially was not able to complete their bowel prep. The colon is actually filled because nothing was able to pass. And in these cases, a diverting ileostomy leaving a colon filled with stool isn't really diverted.
The surgeon ties off the colotomy and proceeds to suspend the uterus in preparation for the bowel resection.
The surgeon observes the adherence of the colon to the left adnexa and begins dissection from medial to lateral, starting underneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon identifies the left colic artery branching from the superior hemorrhoidal artery, planning to preserve it while performing lateral mobilization of the colon.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the superior hemorrhoidal artery, performing medial dissection before proceeding to lateral dissection.

The surgeon addresses the adherence of the colon to the left adnexa, noting the large and redundant colon which negates the need for extensive lateral dissection or flexure mobilization. Urethral stents are used to aid in the dissection process, particularly in cases with diverticular strictures that are scarred against the left sidewall.
The surgeon completes the dissection and resection, finalizing the separation of the colon from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs a standard sigmoid resection, examining the planned distal margin and bowel caliber. Further rectal dissection is conducted to facilitate reaching the anastomosis and transecting the rectal mesentery. The proximal mesentery is then addressed.

I feel that preserving the left colic and as many of the sigmoidal branches for these patients where you're concerned about vascular supply is beneficial. I really have no evidence to support that, especially in the day and age of ICG, if you trust ICG.
The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to assess the vascular supply of the bowel, highlighting its chronic dilation. Two 60mm staplers are used to transect the dilated bowel segment.

The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis on the rectal side, using a two-layer technique. The outer row is sewn with 2-0 PDS sutures, starting at the confluence of the two staple lines on the proximal colon side. The proximal colon is offset to the patient's left to ensure proper alignment for the end-to-end anastomosis.

I've switched to two-O PDS because I have felt that the three-O V-lock might not be an adequate outer layer and two-O PDS is one of my favorite sutures for my open anastomosis as well as for fascial closure. I have a lot of trust in it, so I'm starting to use that now for my outer layers of my anastomosis. It is slightly harder to work with, just given the lack of the barbs and the need for a longer length to tie knots, but at the very least, with a robotic platform, it doesn't really add too much of a challenge.

The surgeon creates collatomies, ensuring similar sizes on both ends, and checks the ICG fluorescence to assess the perfusion of the inner lip of the anastomosis. The inner row is sewn using a three-oh V-lock suture, with the outer row intact behind it.

We come across, I put a little specimen down in there, now we come across and get our next suture to start and posterior midline. Overall, I've been very happy with how this case has gone. It was very helpful that this was a thin female patient with a nice wide pelvis. The complexity of the case really wasn't that challenging. It was more just decision making. And again, I did elect to make an underverted anastomosis. I did not leave a drain. And the patient did very well. Of course, if the outcome was different. I would blame my decision for having even made it in anastomosis.

I think that for all of us at a certain point in our career, we just start to get comfortable. But I also think that from my own personal experience, seeing videos of similar cases done by other surgeons where they did something that I personally wouldn't have done at that point in my career, did give me the confidence to go ahead and try it myself as well as the discussions on this group and others where people have watched my videos and said nice case no need for an ostomy go ahead and make an anastomosis so um Although this might not have been a case in the past where I would have done what you're seeing me do here by making an anastomosis in this heavy smoker with a chronically obstructed proximal bowel and the size discrepancy, it is something that I'm very comfortable with now. That is not to say that this patient isn't a higher risk for a leak, and this was not one of my patients selected for same day or early discharge. I kept them a full three days.

The surgeon uses a two-O PDS suture to complete the outer anterior row of the anastomosis, tying it to the posterior PDS to ensure a complete two-layer anastomosis. The surgical field is cleaned, and the surgeon checks the anastomosis from the posterior aspect for any need to over-sew. A leak test is performed, showing a widely patent anastomosis with no bleeding or bubbles. The specimen is prepared for extraction, and the uterus is repositioned.
